Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd have bursts where I'd study for hours, usually when I felt guilty about missing classes or when something finally clicked for me. I learn best by doing things with my hands, so I'd often skip the online stuff and just practice problems or make physical flashcards. Some weeks I'd put in close to 30 hours, other weeks barely anything. There wasn't really a system, just whatever I felt I could manage that day.
Q2: It varied a lot depending on how I was feeling emotionally and what deadlines were coming up. I'd go through phases where I'd be really productive for a few days, then completely crash and not touch anything for a week. When assignments were due, I'd suddenly kick into gear, but regular weekly studying? That wasn't really my thing. My extracurriculars also threw off any attempt at a consistent schedule.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or realizing I'd missed something important. Sometimes a classmate would mention something in the discussion forums and I'd log in to see what was going on. I rarely logged in just to browse or stay on top of things proactively. It was more reactive—like, oh no, there's something due tomorrow, better check what it is.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never felt natural to me. I'd go through stretches where I'd force myself to check it more regularly, but it never stuck. Without reliable internet at home, it was always this extra effort to find somewhere to log in, which made it feel like a chore rather than a habit. The times I was most active were when I was on campus for my extracurricular activities anyway.
Q5: The discussion posts were actually helpful when people were genuinely engaging with the material. I liked seeing how others approached problems because I could learn from their different perspectives. The videos were okay but I rarely watched them all the way through—I'd skip to parts that seemed relevant. Anything that let me interact or do something practical felt more useful than just reading.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and longer lecture videos. Sitting and passively watching or reading just doesn't work for my brain—I zone out. I'd often download things with good intentions but never actually open them. The resources that required me to just absorb information without doing anything felt like a waste of time, even though I know they probably weren't.
Q7: If I could see an immediate connection to what I needed to do for an assignment or exam, it felt worth it. Activities where I could actually practice something or get feedback made sense to me. But if something felt theoretical or disconnected from the actual assessments, I'd question why I was spending time on it. I needed to feel like I was accomplishing something tangible.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could ignore, especially when I was stressed or sad. Required ones at least had a deadline that forced me to engage. With optional stuff, I'd tell myself I'd get to it later, but later never came. There was no external pressure, and honestly, my internal motivation wasn't strong enough to pick up the slack most days.
Q9: All the time. I'd download PDFs and work through them on paper, making notes and diagrams. I had a study group from my extracurricular activities where we'd meet in person and work through problems together on whiteboards. I also used textbooks from the library a lot. Most of my actual learning happened away from screens, which is just how I process things best.
Q10: My study group sessions were probably the most valuable learning I did, but none of that showed up anywhere. We'd spend hours working through concepts together, explaining things to each other, drawing diagrams. I'd also do a lot of practice problems on paper that I never submitted anywhere. The LMS would just show I was barely there, but I was actually working hard in my own way.
Q11: Yeah, constantly. I'd open a resource once to download it, then work from the downloaded version for days or weeks. Or I'd take screenshots of important slides and study from those on my phone when I didn't have internet. The initial access was just me grabbing what I needed to study offline. My actual engagement with that material happened completely outside the system.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when everything piled up and I was feeling really down. I'd miss classes and then feel too embarrassed or overwhelmed to catch up. The sadness would hit and I'd just... disengage from everything. My CBT sessions helped me recognize these patterns, but in the moment, it was hard to push through when I felt like I was already too far behind.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or stressed, logging into the LMS felt like climbing a mountain. I'd look at all the things I'd missed and feel paralyzed instead of motivated. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars sometimes meant I had to choose, and the LMS often lost. Confusion about what was actually required versus optional also made me avoid it—I didn't want to waste energy on the wrong things.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind wandered somewhere else entirely. Or I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just to feel like I was doing something. When I was in a low mood, I could spend an hour on the LMS and retain absolutely nothing. My body was there but my brain had checked out.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even the ones I knew were important for my grade. Part of it was anxiety—starting something meant risking failure, and that felt worse than just not starting. Part of it was the sadness making everything feel pointless. I'd tell myself I worked better under pressure anyway, but really I was just avoiding the discomfort of engaging when I felt so low.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to use the LMS consistently. I'd have these intense bursts of activity right before something was due, cramming everything into a short window. Without a deadline, I'd drift. The structure of due dates was like an external motivation system since my internal one was pretty unreliable. It wasn't healthy, but it's how I functioned.
Q17: When I got good feedback, especially on exams, it reminded me I could actually do this. That would boost my engagement for a little while. But if feedback was delayed or vague, I'd lose interest before it even came. The disconnect between effort and feedback timing made it hard to feel like my engagement mattered. I needed that immediate reinforcement to stay motivated.
Q18: The structure was both helpful and frustrating. Having clear deadlines gave me something to work toward, but the expectation of regular attendance and consistent engagement didn't match how I actually learn. I do better with flexibility—intense focus periods followed by breaks. The course assumed steady participation, which just isn't me, especially when I'm struggling emotionally.
Q19: The lack of hands-on activities made it harder for me as a kinesthetic learner. I needed to do things, not just watch or read. When there were interactive elements or practical applications, I engaged more. But a lot of the course was passive consumption, which my brain just rejects. Also, not having reliable internet made everything dependent on the LMS feel like an extra barrier.
Q20: I think sometimes you're searching for something specific and can't find it, so you click around a lot. Or you're anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually accomplishing anything. For me, high activity sometimes meant I was lost and confused, not that I was engaged. Activity doesn't equal productivity—sometimes it's just digital pacing because you don't know what else to do.
Q21: That's literally me. My exam scores were really good even though my LMS activity was inconsistent. I studied in ways that worked for my learning style—hands-on practice, study groups, working through problems on paper. The LMS was just one tool, and honestly not the most effective one for me. Low online activity doesn't mean low effort or low understanding, just different methods.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when you're active and what you click on, but they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're actually learning or just going through the motions. They definitely miss all the offline studying, the group work, the thinking you do while walking to class. They capture presence but not understanding or effort.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting genuine mental effort into understanding it. It's not about how many times you log in or how long you watch videos. For me, engagement looks like getting absorbed in a problem, discussing ideas with classmates, feeling curious about the material. It's an internal state that external metrics can't really capture.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled emotionally this semester, or how my sadness affected my ability to engage even when I wanted to. It wouldn't show the hours I spent studying offline, or the support I got from my study group and CBT sessions. It wouldn't show that despite my messy attendance and inconsistent participation, I actually understood the material deeply. The data tells a story, but it's not my whole story.
